coredata.dc:title: The use of carbon-based nanomaterials conjugated to cobalt phthalocyanine complex in the electrochemical detection of nitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Ndebele, Nobuhle
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the non-covalent conjugation of carbon-based nanomaterial with tris(4-tert-butylphenoxy)-(5-phenoxylpicolinic acid) phthalocyanato cobalt (II) (CoPc). The CoPc was π-stacked onto the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) and the single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) yielding CoPc@GQDs, CoPc@NGQDs and CoPc@SWCNTs, respectively. The hybrids were characterised using various analytical techniques. The CoPc and conjugates were studied electrochemically and used as electrocatalysts in the electrooxidation/detection of nitrite. The sensitivities (3–28 μAμM−1), catalytic rate constants (1.8 × 102–5.42 × 104 M−1 s−1) and limits of detection (0.17–1.70 μM) obtained herein show that the electrocatalysts have good nitrite detection capabilities, comparable to those in literature.

head.ce:title: The use of carbon-based nanomaterials conjugated to cobalt phthalocyanine complex in the electrochemical detection of nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the non-covalent conjugation of carbon-based nanomaterial with tris(4-tert-butylphenoxy)-(5-phenoxylpicolinic acid) phthalocyanato cobalt (II) (CoPc). The CoPc was π-stacked onto the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) and the single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) yielding CoPc@GQDs, CoPc@NGQDs and CoPc@SWCNTs, respectively. The hybrids were characterised using various analytical techniques. The CoPc and conjugates were studied electrochemically and used as electrocatalysts in the electrooxidation/detection of nitrite. The sensitivities (3–28 μAμM−1), catalytic rate constants (1.8 × 102–5.42 × 104 M−1 s−1) and limits of detection (0.17–1.70 μM) obtained herein show that the electrocatalysts have good nitrite detection capabilities, comparable to those in literature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalocyanine complexes have been extensively used in various applications ranging from energy storage, non-linear optics, photodynamic therapy, catalysis and sensing [1–5]. These molecules owe their practical applications to their remarkable chemical, photochemical and electrochemical properties [6–8]. In this work, we employ a phthalocyanine complex as an electrocatalyst in electrochemical sensing. The fundamental properties that allow these complexes to be used in several applications can be enhanced (altered) by adding substituents at the α or β positions on the ring and inserting a metal in the central cavity of the complex [6,9,10]. The insertion of a metal results in the formation of metallophthalocyanines (MPc). MPcs that contain electroactive central metals such as cobalt, iron, and manganese are commonly used for electrochemical sensing [11]. Incorporating nanomaterials can further improve the chemical and electrochemical properties of MPcs [12].

This work explores the use of carbon nanomaterials with a cobalt phthalocyanine complex for electrochemical sensing using nitrite as a test analyte. Graphene-based nanostructures have been proven to improve conductivity [13,14], increase surface area [15], provide stability [16] and retain the activity of the molecules they are linked to [14]. In this work, we compare the effects of different graphene based nanomaterials on the electrocatalytic activity of a CoPc. The nanomaterials employed are: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). They are each used together with a CoPc (Fig. 1 ) for sensing in this work.

Graphene based nanomaterials can be attached to the MPc through π-π stacking [17] since both contain π-electrons. π-π stacking is a non-covalent interaction that occurs on planar structures such as aromatic rings and graphene via their sp2 hybridised atoms [17,18]. Although non-covalent “bonds” are relatively weaker compared to covalent bonds, the non-covalent linkage is advantageous as it does not change the molecular structure [19]. We have reported on electrochemical sensing using Pcs in the presence of GQDs, NGQDs [20–22] and SWCNT [23], but mainly symmetric Pcs were employed. Asymmetry is known to improve electrocatalysis in porphyrin-type complexes [24]. Hence we employ an asymmetrical phthalocyanine in this work. The asymmetric CoPc employed in this work has been reported [25] and linked to metal tungstate nanoparticles and used for sensing. In this work, we use π-π interactions to link the CoPc to the carbon based nanoparticles. As stated above, π-π linkage preserves the structure of the molecule.

One CoPc derivative is studied together with different carbon nanomaterials for the first time in order to investigate the effect of the nanomaterials and also explore how the structure (planar sheet vs rolled) and doping of the nanomaterials affects the electrochemical properties of CoPc.

Nitrite is employed as a test analyte in this work. Nitrite is an intermediate oxidation state between nitrate and ammonia [26,27]. It is a naturally occurring component of the nitrogen cycle, readily found in humans and certain foods such as fruits and vegetables [26]. Nitrite can also be added to processed foods to combat the growth of harmful bacteria while adding flavour [28]. Despite its application in industrial systems and its benefits, at high concentrations, nitrite poses a significant threat to plant and aquatic life, in humans it has been associated with various ailments and diseases [29,30], hence the constant search for sensors that can detect it at trace levels. Compared to traditional analytical strategies, electrochemical sensing is used herein due to its simplicity, sensitivity, cost-effectiveness [31,32]. The use of phthalocyanines and nanomaterials in the electrochemical detection of nitrite has been reported [33–35]. In this work we use asymmetrical CoPc with different types of carbon nanomaterials.

Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7) was prepared using; NaCl (137 mM), KCl (2.7 mM), Na2HPO4 (10 mM) and KH2PO4 (1.8 mM) in Type II Elga water was obtained from an Elga PURE LAB Chorus 2 (Ro/di) system. The single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were purchased from Nanolab. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared according to literature [36]. The synthesis of tris(4-tert-butylphenoxy)-(5-phenoxylpicolinic acid) phthalocyanato cobalt (II) have been previously reported [25].

Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2250 spectrophotometer. The Raman spectral data was collected using the Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen-cooled germanium detector). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector (proportional counter) using Cu Kα radiation (l = 1.5405 A°, nickel filter) as described before in the literature [37]. The thermal decomposition profiles were recorded over a temperature range of 50–700 °C on a Perkin Elmer TGA 8000 Thermogravimetric Analyzer. A Zeiss Libra 120 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 80 kV and an INCA PENTA FET coupled to the VAGA TESCAM using 20 kV accelerating voltage were used to record transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs.

All electrochemical experiments: cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using the Autolab potentiostat PGSTAT 302 electrochemical workstation (driven by NOVA software). A three-electrode electrochemical cell system that consists of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and the silver| silver chloride wire (Ag|AgCl) (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode was employed. EIS experiments were performed between 0.1 Hz and 10 KHz using a 5 mV rms sinusoidal modulation.

The cobalt phthalocyanine complex was conjugated to the nanomaterials via non-covalent bonding, using their π-electrons; this method was adapted from literature [38]. A stock solution of the CoPc (30 mg, 2.60 × 10−2 mmol) was prepared by dissolving it in dimethyl formamide (DMF, 6 mL). While in parallel vessels, 5 mg of the respective nanomaterials were dissolved in 1 mL (water for NGQDs and GQDs and DMF for SWCNTs), followed by sonication for 24 h at room temperature. Then, CoPc solution (2 mL) was added to each reaction vessel containing the nanomaterial, and the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for approximately 96 h under inert conditions to yield π-stacked conjugates. The conjugates were washed in a water-ethanol mixture (50:50 v/v), and precipitated in ethanol, and dried in an inert vacuum. These conjugates are represented as CoPc@GQDs, CoPc@NGQDs and CoPc@SWCNTs.
The GCE surfaces were polished on a Buehler-felt polishing pad in an alumina nanopowder (˂10 μm) slurry. The electrodes were rinsed several times using Elga Type II purified water.

The working electrodes were modified using the conventional drop-dry method, where 1 mg (in 1 mL DMF) of the respective electrocatalysts were deposited onto the electrode surface and dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. The six electrodes are represented as GCE/GQDs, GCE/CoPc@GQDs, GCE/NGQDs, GCE/CoPc@NGQDs, GCE/SWCNTs and GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs.

The synthesis and characterisation of the cobalt phthalocyanine complex and the two carbon-based nanomaterials (GQDs and NGQDs) used in this work were previously reported on [25,36]. SWCNTs were purchased.

Herein, conjugation of the cobalt phthalocyanine complex onto the nanomaterials was achieved via non-covalent bonding. This conjugation is achieved through the interaction of the π-electrons from respective nanomaterials [38]. An aliquot of the CoPc complex was sonicated and stirred with an aliquot of the individual nanomaterials for approximately 96 h under inert conditions at room temperature to afford the conjugates; CoPc@GQDS, CoPc@NGQDs and CoPc@SWCNTs. These conjugates were characterised using various analytical techniques. Fig. S1 shows the TEM images of the synthesized GQDs and NGQDs. The nanoparticles are monodispersed.

Fig. 2 illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectra obtained from CoPc alone and when conjugated with the nanomaterials. Upon conjugation, a red shift in the Q-band was observed. CoPc displayed a Q-band absorption maxima at 659 nm, while the Q-bands of the conjugates were observed at 663, 664 and 667 nm for CoPc@GQDs, CoPc@NGQDs and CoPc@SWCNTs, respectively, Table 1 . The Q-bands of MPcs are sensitive to π-π interactions; π-stacking of the MPcs and nanomaterial results in the formation of conjugates in the ground state, hence changes in the absorption maxima were observed [39]. The increase in the B-band intensity is typical for MPcs in the presence of nanomaterials, as the nanomaterials used in this work absorb in this region between 320 and 390 nm (Fig. 2b).

Raman spectroscopy was used to study the graphitic framework of the nanomaterials used in this work. Fig. 3 is an illustration of the Raman spectra of the nanomaterials obtained before and after conjugation.

The G bands observed in the Raman spectra are attributed to the in-plane vibrations from the sp2 hybridised carbon atoms. In contrast, the D bands are due to the out-of-plane vibrations from scattering structural defects associated with the sp3 carbon atoms [40,41]. The G and D bands for GQDS were observed at 1587 cm−1 and 1354 cm−1, respectively, and for NGQDs at 1605 cm−1 and 1171 cm−1, respectively. Upon conjugation, there were slight shifts of the G and D bands to relatively lower wavenumbers (< 4 cm−1) at 1585 cm−1 and 1357 cm−1 for CoPc@GQDs and at 1602 cm−1 and 1170 cm−1 for NGQDs, respectively. Although a decrease in the wavenumber upon conjugation was observed for GQDs and NGQDs, an increase was observed for SWCNTs. SWCNTs had a third prominent peak known as the 2D peak. This peak is an overtone of the D band [42]. After conjugation, the G band shifted to higher wavenumbers by 4 cm−1 from 1600 cm−1 to 1604 cm−1. The D and 2D shifted to higher wavenumbers by 5 and 2 cm−1, respectively. The shifts in the G bands are an indication of strong interaction CoPc and the nanomaterials [40].

The ID/IG ratios were calculated to determine the degree of the defects due to the disruption in the graphitic framework caused by conjugation (if any) [41]. After conjugating Pcs to carbon nanomaterials, their graphitic framework is disrupted and therefore, an increase in the ID/IG ratio is expected [41]. Table 1, shows an increase in the ID/IG ratio for all conjugates compared to nanomaterials alone.
The reported [43,44] DLS sizes of the GQDs and NGQDs are shown in Table 1. The GQDs are larger than the NGQDs.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to study the morphology and crystallinity of the nanomaterials used in this work. Fig. 4 is an illustration of the X-ray powder diffractogram of the NPs and conjugates recorded from 2θ from 5° to 100°. The XRD diffractogram of CoPc has been published [25], showing a broad peak near 20°. This peak is observed in the diffractograms obtained for the conjugates. However, this peak might overlap with that of the nanomaterials as graphene structures have prominent peaks at ≈ 25° and 43 °C. These peaks are attributed to the 002 and 101 Braggs reflection of the carbon on the structure's graphitic layer [45,46].

Thermographic analysis (TGA) was used to study the physical and chemical changes of the studied materials. Fig. S2 illustrates the thermograms/decay curves obtained from 50 °C to 700 °C. The results show that the nanomaterials alone were relatively more stable than CoPc and the conjugates. This is because of their structural composition, graphene is thermally stable as it comprises of sp2 hybridised carbons held together via covalent bonds and van de Waal forces which are strong and not easily decomposed (reduced) [47]. GQDs were the most stable nanomaterial as they had only degraded by about 50 % at 700 °C, with CoPc@SWCNTs showing the lowest thermal stability with approximately 90 % degradation at 700 °C. NGQDs are less stable than GQDs because the additional nitrogen atoms readily decompose, compromising the nanomaterials' thermal stability.

TGA was further used to calculate the loading of the nanomaterials onto the phthalocyanine complex. Loading was conducted using a thermal decomposition method described in literature [48]. The loading values were calculated by comparing the thermal decomposition of CoPc alone (before conjugation) with that of the conjugated CoPc. The calculated loading values are tabulated in Table 1. CoPc@GQDs had the highest loading (highest amount of nanomaterials π-π stacked onto the Pc complex), most likely due to the larger size of GQDs compared to NGQDs.
The modified electrodes were characterised using various techniques to aid in determining parameters such as conductivity, charge resistance and surface coverage.

Herein, cyclic voltammetry was used to determine if the electrode surface modification was successful and determine the electron transfer ability of the individual electrocatalysts. CV was used to obtain full profiles and obtain both anodic and cathodic peaks. Fig. 5 is an illustration of the cyclic voltammograms obtained in a) the ferrocyanide redox couple solution ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl) and b) in the phosphate buffered saline solution at pH 7.

Fig. 5a shows that each electrocatalyst exhibited distinctive reduction-oxidation properties as they all displayed unique responses to the redox couple in terms of both the currents and peak potentials despite being subjected to the same conditions. Based on this, it can be concluded that each electrocatalyst has different electron transfer capabilities and abilities. Fig. 5a was used to calculate the electrocatalysts' peak-to-peak separations (ΔEp). A low ΔEp indicates good electron transfer and is highly preferred in electrochemistry [49].

The ΔEp values herein range between 54 mV and 346 mV (Table 2 ). The unmodified GCE had a peak-to-peak separation of 63 mV which is considered a good value. A low ΔEp value for the bare GCE is not only synonymous with desirable electron-transporting abilities but is also often associated with a clean electrode surface. Upon modification of the electrode surface, the ΔEp values increased for all the studied electrocatalysts, with the exception of GQDs (54 mV). ΔEp is likely to increase after modification as the electrode surface would have been blocked by the electrocatalysts [50]. The conjugated electrocatalysts had relatively higher ΔEp values compared to the nanomaterials alone. This implies that the nanomaterials exhibited better electron transfer capabilities in the redox coupled solution. GQDs improved the electron transfer of CoPc as judged by the decrease in ΔEp value for CoPc@GQDs. CoPc@NGQDs only displayed a cathodic peak therefore the ΔEp value could not be calculated.

The voltammograms in Fig. 5a were also determined the effective electrode surface area and surface coverage. The surface coverage represents the amount of the electrocatalyst on the electrode surface, which determines electrocatalytic efficiency. The data obtained from Fig. 5a was fitted into the Randles-Sevick equation [1]. The equation is used for reversible systems applied on the [Fe(CN) 6]3−/4− redox couple [51].

where; Ip represents the peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred (≈ 1), and v is the scan rate (V s−1). A, D and C represent the electrode surface area (cm−2), the diffusion coefficient (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 [52]) of [Fe(CN) 6]3−/4− and the concentration of the redox couple, respectively.

The calculated effective electrode surface areas were further used to calculate the respective surface coverages. The calculated effective electrode areas were fitted into Eq. 2 [53].
(2)
Г =
Q nFA
where Г, n, F, A and Q are respectively, the surface coverage, the number of electrons transferred (≈1), Faraday's constant, the effective electrode area and the total charge of the electrocatalysts in the buffer. The charge of the electrocatalysts was obtained by manipulating the data from Fig. 5b using the peaks labelled I. The peaks near 0.5 V are due to CoIIIPc/CoIIPc in CoPc containing electrodes [54]. Peaks in this region have been reported for GQDs [55]. For SWCNTs, the peak near 0 V was employed [56]. The surface coverage values obtained are summarised in Table 2, where it can be observed that SWCNTs and their conjugate had the largest surface coverage, with NGQDs accounting for the lowest surface coverage. Carbon nanotubes generally have a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio and a larger electrochemically active surface area compared to other nanomaterials, this is the reason why SWCNTs exhibited a higher surface coverage [57]. Γ values are lower than or close to the 1 × 10−10 mol/cm2 ascribed to Pcs assuming a monolayer on the electrode surface where the Pc molecules are in a flat position [58], thus suggesting a monolayer arrangement in this work.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used as a qualitative technique to determine each electrocatalyst's conductivity and electron transfer ability by studying their respective charge resistance values [59]. Fig. S2a illustrates the Nyquist plots obtained from the EIS data. The EIS data was fitted using an equivalent Randles electric circuit [59]. This circuit is used in diffusion-controlled electrochemical systems and consists of the solution resistance (Rs), constant phase element (CPE), Warburg element and the electron transfer resistance (Rct), as illustrated in Fig. S2b [59].

The Rct values obtained are summarised in Table 2. A low Rct is indicative of good electron transfer ability [59]. Herein, the lowest Rct value was obtained for the bare electrode. The Rct values increase after modification due to blockage of the electrode surface by the electrocatalyst immobilized on the electrode surface [59]. Of the modified GCE, the lowest Rct value was calculated for GQDs, followed by CoPc@GQDs indicating that they had the lowest charge transfer resistance, and therefore, the best electron transfer abilities in ferrocyanide at the electrode surface. CoPc@SWCNTs exhibited the highest Rct value. A high Rct value can be attributed to adsorption at the electrode surface, therefore, hindering the diffusion of electrons [59]. To some extent, the trend in the Rct values follows the ΔEp in that the electrode with the lowest Rct values are obtained for bare GCE and GQDS, which also have the lowest ΔEp values. The CoPc@SWCNTs with the largest ΔEp value also have the largest Rct value, Table 2.

Cyclic voltammetry was used to study the electrocatalytic behavior of the electrocatalysts in the presence of nitrite and to determine if the synthesized electrocatalysts can detect nitrite and, if so, to what extent. Fig. 6 is an illustration of the cyclic voltammograms obtained for each electrocatalyst in 2 mM nitrite solution in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). These studies were carried out in a pH 7 buffer because the maximum faradic oxidation current for nitrite was recorded at pH 7 on a GCE modfied with FePc derivative [60]. This pH 7 is also appropriate for future real applications.

From the figure, it can be observed that each electrocatalyst has unique nitrite detection abilities this is because they all display different nitrite oxidation peak potentials and oxidation currents. The following observations were drawn from the cyclic voltammograms in Fig. 6 (Table 2).

The oxidation peak potential was the lowest and most favourable for CoPc alone compared to the other modified electrodes. The conjugated electrocatalysts exhibited relatively higher oxidation potentials compared to their respective nanoparticles alone. The electrocatalysts are ranked as follows with respect to their oxidation peak potentials; CoPc < GQDs ≅ NGQDs < SWCNTS < CoPc@SWCNTs < CoPc@NGQDs ≅ CoPc@GQDs. Considering that the CoPc exhibited the lowest oxidation peak potential and that the nanomaterials alone had lower potentials compared to their conjugates, it can be assumed that conjugation did not improve the nitrite detection/oxidation peak potentials. The nitrite oxidation peak potentials obtained in this work are comparable with those obtained in literature, which ranged between 0.65 V and 0.92 V [33–35]. CoPc exhibited the lowest and least favourable oxidation current (7 μA), indicating that CoPc had a relatively low sensitivity rate to nitrite. The current for CoPc increased in the presence of nanomaterials, thus the nanomaterials improved the catalytic activity of CoPc. According to literature, graphene has a higher electronic conductivity making graphene-based nanomaterials more sensitive compared to their counterparts [33,34]. In this work, it is observed that the planar graphene material (GQDs and NGQDs) exhibited higher oxidation currents compared to the rolled-up graphene sheet (SWCNTs), even though the latter had higher surface coverage. This is attributed to their structure; change in the chirality, and structural defects that occur when rolling the graphene, affecting the properties [35,61,62]. NGQDs and their conjugates were expected to exhibit a higher sensitivity (oxidation current) than GQDs as doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, is expected to enhance the electrocatalytic properties of the materials and this should be prevalent in the oxidation peak current, as a rough measure of sensitivity [63,64]. However, in this case, the addition of the hetero atom did not enhance nitrite oxidation (detection) properties in terms of oxidation currents.

Chronoamperometry was used to study the reaction kinetics and determine the detection limits. Chronoamperometry was employed since it has been reported to show high signal-to-noise ratio compared to differential pulse voltammetry [65]. Chronoamperometry involves the study of current as a function of time at a specified potential. The potential applied for each electrocatalyst was the oxidation peak potential obtained from the cyclic voltammograms in Section 3.3.1 Fig. 7a is an illustration of the chronoamperograms obtained for GCE/CoPc@GQDs. From the figure, it can be noted that an increase in the faradaic oxidation current was observed upon the increase in nitrite concentration (0–1000 μM). GCE/CoPc@GQDs were used as an example since all chronoamperograms obtained in this work exhibited a similar trend.

The limits of detection (LoD) were calculated using the calibration curve inserted in Fig. 7a and Eq. (3). The calibration curve was obtained by plotting the concentration vs the current obtained at 5 s from the chronoamperograms of each electrocatalyst. When calculating the LoD, the slope of the calibration curve (electrocatalyst sensitivity) was used in Eq. (3).
(3)
LoD =
3 δ
S
where S is the slope of the calibration curve, and δ represents the standard deviation of 30 chronoamperograms run in the pH 7 buffer. Table 3 summarises the sensitivities and the limits of detection obtained from the electrocatalysts studied herein. The tabulated results show that the conjugates outperformed the nanoparticles and the cobalt phthalocyanine (as individual entities) as the conjugates exhibited relatively lower detection limits and higher sensitivities with the exception of SWCNTs and their conjugate (Table 3). GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs showed a higher detection limit than GCE/SWCNTs. The detection limit calculated for GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs (1.01 μM) was higher than that of GCE/SWCNTs (0.44 μM), being the second highest LoD calculated in this work after GCE/CoPc at 1.7 μM. CoPc@GQDs, CoPc@NGQDs, and CoPc@SWCNTs have lower detection limits than CoPc alone, showing that nanomaterials improve the sensing abilities of CoPc.

The lowest and least favourable sensitivity (3.16 μA.μM−1) was also calculated for GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs, showing that GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs had relatively unimpressive nitrite detection abilities. The results show that GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs were not sensitive to nitrite detection and displayed poor electrocatalytic properties. Nitrite's response to electrooxidation (detection) in this work shows that it is readily oxidised by planar materials (GQDs) as opposed to bulkier material (SWCNTs). An extensive review written by Li and colleagues, on the detection of nitrite using carbon-based nanomaterials shows that planar graphene-based nanomaterials, such as quantum dots, were more sensitive towards nitrite compared to single-walled carbon nanotubes achieving lower detection limits and relatively higher sensitivities as obtained in this work [66]. This is due to the fact that graphene quantum dots have relatively; (i) more electrochemically active sites, (ii) higher surface areas and (iii) stronger electrocatalytic activity, which make them more sensitive and selective towards the detection of nitrite [66]. The LoDs and detection sensitivities obtained in this work are comparable or even better in some cases compared to those obtained in literature [33–35,67], Table 3.

The catalytic rates of the oxidation of nitrite using the electrocatalysts were calculated by manipulating the data obtained from the chronoamperograms in Fig. 7a to obtain Fig. 7b and c. Fig. 7b is an illustration of a linear plot of the ratio of the oxidation current obtained from the concentration of nitrite (Icat) over the oxidation current of the buffer (Ibla) versus the square root of the time lapsed. These currents were obtained from Fig. 7a. The gradients obtained from the linear pots in Fig. 7b were fitted into Eq. (4) and used to calculate the catalytic rate constants. Eq. (4) has been described in literature and used to determine the catalytic rate constant [68].

where C, k and t represent the concentration of nitrite, catalytic rate constant and time lapsed, respectively. The gradients (slopes) obtained from the calibration curves in Fig. 7b were squared and plotted against the studied (respective) concentrations to afford the linear curve in Fig. 7c. The slopes obtained in Fig. 7c were then used to calculate the catalytic rate of each electrocatalyst towards the oxidation of nitrite. The linear equations obtained for the electrocatalysts (from Fig. 7c plots) are represented in Eq. (5).

The calculated catalytic rate constants are summarised in Table 3. GCE/GQDs and GCE/CoPc exhibited the lowest catalytic rate constants, while GCE/SWCNTs and the respective conjugate had the highest catalytic rate constant, this is attributed to SWCNTs being relatively stable in the buffer solution compared to the other electrocatalysts.

In this work, kinetic studies were conducted to determine the reaction kinetics, parameters and mechanisms of the electrocatalyst towards the electrooxidation of nitrite. These experiments were conducted by varying the scan rates while keeping each electrocatalyst's concentration and potential window constant. Cyclic voltammetry was used to conduct this study.

Fig. 8a illustrates the cyclic voltammograms obtained for GCE/GQDs and GCE/CoPc@GQDs at different scan rates. Herein cyclic voltammetry scans were conducted at scan rates ranging from 10 mVs−1 to 100 mVs−1. An increase in the oxidation current with a shift in the oxidation potential to the more positive values as the scan rate increases confirms the irreversibility of the oxidation process.

Fig. 8b represents the linear relationship between the oxidation peak current and the square root of the corresponding scan rate (data taken from Fig. 8a). Fig. 8b is used to determine the reaction mechanism between nitrite and the electrocatalysts on the electrode surface. The linearity suggests an irreversible diffusion-controlled reaction occurring at the electrode surface [69]. Eqs. (6)–(8) show the proposed mechanism for the electrooxidation of nitrite involving cobalt phthalocyanine complexes [70].

Studies have shown that cobalt phthalocyanine complexes do interact with nitrite ions in aqueous media [71], hence Eq. (6) is proposed. The mechanism of nitrite oxidation is well-studied and understood. Eqs. (9) and (10) summarise the reaction mechanism illustrating the deprotonation of nitrite, which subsequently leads to the formation of nitrate ions and nitric oxide [72,73].

Tafel slopes are a graphical point used to measure the efficiency with which an electrode can produce a current in response to the change in potential [74]. Herein, Tafel slopes were calculated using Fig. 8c, a linear plot representing the oxidation peak potential against the log of the scan rate. The data from Fig. 8c was fitted into Eq. (11) to calculate the Tafel slopes [75].
(11)
E p
=
b 2
log v + K
where b is equivalent to the Tafel slope, and K is a constant. Tafel slopes between 60 and 120 mV/decade are highly favourable as they indicate a one-electron transfer process during the rate-determining step, where the electron transfer coefficient (α) = 1 [76]. The Tafel slopes obtained in this work range between 138 mV/decade – 563 mV/decade (Table 1). An increase in the Tafel slope was observed upon conjugation. Tafel slopes above 120 mV/decade have no kinetic meaning and suggest the possibility of chemical complications on the surface of the electrode [54]. However, other factors than the reaction mechanism have been reported to affect the reaction kinetics, resulting in high Tafel slope values [77,78]. Factors such as; electrode surface defects, potential window, solvent environment, temperature and the number of active sites influence the Tafel slope.

Fig. 9 is an illustration of repetitive cyclic voltammetric scans that were conducted under the same conditions (concentration, scan rate and potential window). These scans were conducted to determine the stability of the electrocatalysts in the presence of the analyte and the reproducibility of the results obtained overall. For each electrocatalyst, 20 consecutive scans were run, from the figure, it can be observed that the electrocatalysts were stable, as there were no significant changes observed in the oxidation peak potentials when analysing the multiple scans. The decrease in peak currents were calculated to be 16.81 % (GCE/GQDs), 7.06 % (GCE/CoPc@GQDs), 12.83 % (GCE/NGQDs), 10 % (GCE/CoPc@NGQDs), 27.88 % (GCE/SWCNTs) and 12.16 % (GCE/CoPc@SWCNTs), all peak current decreases were below 30 % indicating that the electrocatalysts were stable in nitrite. The relative standard deviations calculated ranged between 2 and 5.
Interference studies were used to determine the sensitivity of the electrocatalysts towards the detection of nitrite in the presence of other analytes (interfering species), Fig. 10 .

The following ions; NO3 2−, Cl− and SO4 2− were selected and used as the interferants of choice. These ions tend to co-exist with nitrite and are known to influence the detection of nitrite. Fig. 10 illustrates the differential pulse voltammograms (−0.5 V to 1.3 V) obtained in this study. For this particular study, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used as it is a highly sensitive technique (compared to cyclic voltammetry for example) and has the ability to detect substances in trace concentrations. The figure shows that neither of the anionic species displayed a detection peak that overlaps with that of nitrite. Slight changes in the peak potential were observed when nitrite was studied with the anionic species. These changes were, however, below 5 % and are therefore negligible.

This paper reports on the successful conjugation (via π-stacking) of a phthalocyanine complex to carbon-based nanomaterials; graphene quantum dots (CoPc@GQDs), nitrogen-doped quantum dots (CoPc@NGQDs) and single-walled carbon nanotubes (CoPc@SWCNTs). The conjugates were characterised using UV–vis absorption spectra, Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray powder diffraction. The conjugates were used to modify GCE and employed as electrocatalysts. Electrochemical techniques such as; cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectrochemistry and chronoamperometry were used to study the properties of the electrocatalysts and their nitrite detection parameters. Overall, GQDs and conjugate showed the best nitrite detection properties in terms of LoD, and CoPc@SWCNTs had the least favourable properties.
Nobuhle Ndebele: Conceptualisation, visualisation, investigation, methodology and writing. Tebello Nyokong: Project supervision, reviewing and editing.
